oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Managing parasites in beef cattle
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Vatta, Adriano
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: anthelmintic resistance
Ostertagia
Haemonchus
Cooperia,
cattle
gastrointestinal nematodes
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Though limited in their scope, investigations in the southern United States indicate that resistance to the macrocyclic lactone anthelmintics is highly prevalent in Cooperia spp., common in Haemonchus spp., and emerging in Ostertagia ostertagi. This has important implications for sustainable beef farming in the southern United States, but also for operations which purchase calves from the South for finishing in the Midwest and southern Great Plains. It appears that producers generally do not know the efficacy status of the available drug classes in their opera­tions, and there is an overall lack of awareness of resistance in cattle nematodes. This hampers the producer’s ability to adopt sustainable deworming practices. To improve the situ­ation, there is a need for veterinarians to educate their clients about anthelmintic resistance, to encourage the use of fecal egg counts to assess the need for deworming, and to test for anthel­mintic resistance by means of fecal egg count reduction tests. Producers should be educated on the need to employ refugia in control strategies and to use combination anthelmintic treat­ment where possible.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 8-11
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
